retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Pathophysiology -- Theca Lutein Cysts
retrieved_docs.content: Theca lutein cysts are luteinized follicle cysts that form as a result of overstimulation in elevated human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) levels. They can occur in pregnant women, women with gestational trophoblastic disease, multiple gestation, and ovarian hyperstimulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Female Pelvic Pathology Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation  -- Clinical Significance -- Complex Cystic Adnexal Mass
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrioma (chocolate cyst): It is due to localized endometriosis of the ovary. They are varied in appearance, but the most common appearance is a unilocular cyst with acoustic enhancement posterior to the cyst and diffuse homogenous ground glass appearance resulting from hemorrhagic debris. While less typical features are: punctate echogenic wall foci due to calcification or cholesterol crystals. They are usually bilateral, adherent, and occupy the pouch of Douglas. Multiple locules and septations may be present in a few cases. Other sites of endometriotic deposits are cul-de-sac, uterosacral ligaments, uterus, and colon. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Pathophysiology -- Neoplastic Cysts
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial glands and stroma at extrauterine sites, with the ovary being one of the most common sites. Endometriomas (common in endometriosis) arise from ectopic growth of endometrial tissue and are often referred to as chocolate cysts because they contain dark, thick, gelatinous aged blood products. They appear as a complex mass on ultrasound and are described as having “ground glass” internal echoes. Endometriomas can be classified into two types. Type I consists of small primary endometriomas that develop from surface endometrial implants. Type II arises from functional cysts that have been invaded by ovarian endometriosis or type I endometriomas. Although the overall risk of malignant transformation is low, endometriomas increase this risk in women with endometriosis. [22] [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Pathophysiology -- Follicular and Corpus Luteal Cysts
retrieved_docs.content: Follicular and corpus luteal cysts are considered functional or physiologic cysts, and both occur during the normal menstrual cycle. Follicular cysts arise when follicles fail to rupture during ovulation and can appear smooth, thin-walled, and unilocular. In the follicular phase, follicular cysts may form because of a lack of physiological release of the ovum due to excessive FSH stimulation or the absence of the usual LH surge at mid-cycle just before ovulation. These cysts continue to grow because of hormonal stimulation. Follicular cysts are usually larger than 2.5 cm in diameter. Granulosa cells lead to excess estradiol production, which in turn leads to decreased frequency of menstruation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The ovarian cyst has a broad range of differential diagnoses, broadly classified into gynecological and nongynaecological subcategories.

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of the different types of ovarian cysts. Outline the typical presentation and most common physical exam findings for patients presenting with ovarian cysts. Review the criteria for conservative versus surgical management of ovarian cysts. Explain the importance of collaboration and highlight the interprofessional team's role in evaluating and treating patients affected by life-threatening complications of ovarian cysts. retrieved_docs.title: Granulosa Theca Cell Tumors of the Ovary -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic ultrasound and/or contrast-enhanced CT scan are performed to evaluate the abdomen or other sites in patients with suspected metastatic disease. Radiologic studies usually reveal a solid mass with hemorrhagic or fibrotic changes, a multilocular cyst, or an entirely cystic tumor. The key differentiating features from epithelial ovarian tumors are that GCTs are usually confined to the ovary at diagnosis and do not have intracystic papillary projections. Furthermore, the chance of peritoneal spread is low, and GCTs are rarely bilateral. An enlarged uterus with a thickened endometrium is often noted due to an estrogenic effect. Endometrial sampling may detect endometrial hyperplasia in 25% to 50% of patients with GCTs and endometrial carcinoma in another 5% to 10%. In patients with thecoma, carcinoma may be detected in 20% to 25% of patients, and another 15% of patients may have a precursor lesion. [15] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cystadenoma -- Histopathology -- Endometrioid Cystadenoma
retrieved_docs.content: Macroscopic findings: the cysts range in size up to 15 cm. The cyst lining is irregular with areas of hemorrhage. The cyst contents are typically dark brown due to old hemorrhage (chocolate cyst).

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Most pregnancy-associated cysts, both corpus luteal and follicular, resolve spontaneously by 14 to 16 weeks of gestation, allowing conservative management. [31] The resolution of cysts is less likely when larger than 5cm or with complex morphology. Simple cysts smaller than 6 cm have only less than 1% risk of malignancy. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 1st Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the adnexa to either side of the uterus, the right and left ovaries classically lie between the lateral uterine wall and the internal iliac vessels, a relationship more likely altered in multiparous women. The adnexa also includes the fallopian tubes connecting the uterus and ovaries, both of which should be carefully evaluated as they are the most likely locations of ectopic pregnancies. [8] Ovaries classically have a slightly hypoechoic echotexture compared to surrounding tissue and a "chocolate chip cookie" characteristic due to peripheral, anechoic follicles. Large corpus luteum cysts are commonly seen in early pregnancy, typically as a single large, simple cystic structure within ovarian tissue.  Corpus luteum cysts involute as the placenta develops to support the growing fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Pathophysiology -- Follicular and Corpus Luteal Cysts
retrieved_docs.content: Without pregnancy, the life span of the corpus luteum is 14 days. If the egg is fertilized, the corpus luteum continues to secrete progesterone until its dissolution at 14 weeks, when the cysts undergo central hemorrhage. If the dissolution of the corpus luteum does not occur, it may result in a corpus luteal cyst, which usually grows to 3cm. Corpus luteal cysts can appear complex or simple, thick-walled, or contain internal debris. Corpus luteum cysts are always present during pregnancy and usually resolve by the end of the first trimester. Both follicular and corpus luteal cysts can turn into hemorrhagic cysts. They are generally asymptomatic and spontaneously resolve without treatment. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Granulosa Theca Cell Tumors of the Ovary -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient presents with an incidentally detected adnexal mass, the differential diagnosis should consider pelvic masses from gastrointestinal or genitourinary organs, retroperitoneal masses, or cysts. Differential diagnoses of patients with an adnexal mass and abnormal uterine bleeding include benign pathologies such as uterine fibroids and polyps and malignant causes such as uterine cancer with ovarian metastases or epithelial ovarian cancer with metastases to endometrium or a synchronous endometrial and ovarian cancer. Polycystic ovary syndrome and adrenal tumors must be excluded when patients present with a pelvic mass and endocrine abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Granulosa Theca Cell Tumors of the Ovary -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between granulosa theca cell tumors and other ovarian neoplasms based on histological characteristics and molecular markers. Implement evidence-based guidelines for the diagnosis and staging of granulosa theca cell tumors, including the use of imaging modalities and tumor markers. Select the most appropriate surgical approach for patients with granulosa theca cell tumors of the ovary, considering patient age, disease stage, and reproductive desires. Coordinate long-term follow-up care for patients with granulosa theca cell tumors, focusing on monitoring for late effects and recurrence. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Granulosa Theca Cell Tumors of the Ovary -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Granulosa theca cell cancers are ovarian tumors that consist of granulosa cells, theca cells, and fibroblasts in various combinations. Granulosa cells in the sex cords produce sex steroids, and peptides needed for folliculogenesis and ovulation. Granulosa cells also give rise to granulosa cell tumors (GCT), which account for approximately 5% of all ovarian neoplasms and 70% of all sex cord-stromal tumors of the ovary. There are 2 distinct types of GCT, adult and juvenile, based on characteristic clinicopathologic features. The adult type is the most typical variety and manifests in peri- or post-menopausal women. In contrast, the juvenile type constitutes only 5% of cases and occurs in prepubertal girls and young women. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of ovarian cysts or adnexal masses ranges from physiologically normal (follicular or luteal cysts) to ovarian malignancy. [2] Ovarian cysts can occur at any age but are more common in reproductive years and increase in menarchal females due to endogenous hormone production. [3] Simple cysts are the most likely to occur in all age groups, and mixed cystic and solid and completely solid ovarian lesions have a higher rate of malignancy than simple cysts. Although most ovarian cysts are benign, age is the most important independent risk factor, and post-menopausal women with any type of cyst should have proper follow-up and treatment due to a higher risk for malignancy. The ovarian follicle serves as a finding site for “ovarian cysts”, and the cyst itself is located within the ovarian follicle, emphasizing that cystic lesions commonly arise in or are detected in follicular structures. The ovarian cyst is classified as “كيسة مبيضية حميدة”, confirming its typical benign nature and guiding its clinical management. Finally, the ovary is recorded as the location for an “ovarian pregnancy” (“حمل مَبيضِيّ”), a rare ectopic gestation that occurs within ovarian tissue. Collectively, these relationships portray the ovary as a nexus connecting non‑inflammatory and inflammatory disorders, developmental anatomy, surgical considerations, cystic pathology, and unique clinical entities across multiple languages and classification systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesions of the ovary, and noninflammatory disorders of the ovary all share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location for these diverse pathologic processes. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical site of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), an ectopic gestation that occurs within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the ovary in both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory pelvic conditions as well as in rare pregnancy complications, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of ovarian involvement in female pelvic health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2154
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of fallopian tube and ovary and noninflammatory disorder of ovary are gynecologic conditions that share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary anatomical location examined when these disorders are evaluated. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is identified as the location involved in حمل مَبيضِيّ (ovarian pregnancy), an ectopic implantation of a gestation within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize that the ovary serves as a common site for both noninflammatory ovarian pathology and for ovarian ectopic pregnancies, highlighting the need for careful assessment of the ovary in patients presenting with related symptoms or reproductive concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: According to the finding_site_of relationship, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are identified with a finding site in the ovarium. In a similar manner, a Lesion of ovary is described as having a has_finding_site in the ovarium. The ovarium itself is linked by a has_finding_site to اضطراب مبيضي (an ovarian disorder) and by a has_location to حمل مَبيضِيّ (an ovarian pregnancy). These interconnected statements highlight that inflammatory conditions, lesions, and specific disorders of the ovary are all centered on the ovary as the primary anatomical site, and they may be associated with rare presentations such as ovarian pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing the ovary’s role in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: The ovary (ovarium) serves as the primary anatomic site for several gynecologic conditions. Disorders that involve both the fallopian tube and the ovary are identified in the ovary, as are inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs that have a finding site in the ovary. Lesions of the ovary are likewise localized to the ovary. In addition to these pathologic processes, the ovary can be the location of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ). Recognizing the ovary as the common finding site for these disorders and as the potential site of ectopic gestation is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: استئصال قناة فالوب is a surgical intervention that belongs to the broader category of Female Reproductive System Procedures. It is performed in two specific laparoscopic forms: Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral and Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral, both of which are child procedures of استئصال قناة فالوب. Clinically, استئصال قناة فالوب is associated with حمل منتبذ, indicating a relationship between fallopian tube removal and the occurrence or management of ectopic pregnancy. In addition, استئصال قناة فالوب frequently co‑occurs with كيسة مبيضية, reflecting that patients undergoing this procedure often have concurrent ovarian cysts. Recognizing these hierarchical and clinical connections supports comprehensive evaluation and optimal care planning for patients requiring fallopian tube surgery.
